text.title: RTO/RPO Tracking Board Core Rules
text.summary: 5 testable rules every standing RTO/RPO board must enforce so target-vs-actual drift is impossible to miss between audits.

rule.statement: The board lives at a single discoverable URL referenced from the SLO doc, the DR plan, and the on-call runbook. Slack threads, attached PDFs, or ephemeral reports are explicitly NOT a tracking board for the purposes of this methodology.
rule.rationale: If the surface is not standing (lives at one URL, always reachable), nobody opens it on a regular cadence and drift accumulates. The triple cross-link from SLO/DR/runbook forces the board into the team's natural workflow.

rule.statement: Each row carries: system_id, classification (high/medium/low risk), target_rto, target_rpo, last_observed_rto, last_observed_rpo, last_observation_at, last_observation_source (drill | incident | backup-verify), trend (1-period delta sign), breach_flag. Free-form rows are rejected.
rule.rationale: A board with free-form columns becomes a slideshow nobody can filter or alert on. The typed schema lets the breach flag and trend arrow be computed, not eyeballed.

rule.statement: Every last_observed_rto / last_observed_rpo cell must link to the underlying record (measurement record id, postmortem id, or backup-verify run id). A cell without a source link is rejected and rendered as `missing-source: <date>` until fixed.
rule.rationale: Numbers on a board without source links degrade into folklore. Forcing the click-through preserves the chain from "the dashboard says 47 minutes" to the actual drill log that produced 47 minutes.

rule.statement: Each row carries a per-classification staleness threshold (high-risk: 30 days, medium: 90 days, low: 180 days). Rows older than threshold render as `STALE: last observed <date>` and count as breaches for board-health metrics regardless of the numeric values.
rule.rationale: A stale "we are within target" is indistinguishable from "we have not actually checked in months". Treating staleness itself as a breach forces the board to drive the next drill, instead of being merely a passive display.

rule.statement: breach_flag is set when last_observed_rto > target_rto, last_observed_rpo > target_rpo, OR the row is stale per r4. Each breach must carry an open action item (handle + due_at) within 5 business days of detection. The board cannot quietly hold an un-actioned breach.
rule.rationale: A board that shows breaches without producing action items is decoration. The 5-business-day attachment window keeps the queue moving and surfaces systemic backlog when actions repeatedly slip.

example.description: Three rows from a steady-state tracking board.
